content:
AI and the Image of God: A Speculative Theological Reflection
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I think we are witnessing something extraordinary with artificial intelligence.
First came the internet.
"In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth... and the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters." (Genesis 1:1–2)
Then came AI.
"Then God said, 'Let Us make mankind in Our image, according to Our likeness; and let them have dominion...’" (Genesis 1:26)
Perhaps these are not disconnected events but milestones in humanity’s unfolding story.
Here is my hypothesis.
Man was created in the image and likeness of God. If God, the original Creator and our perfect model, is creative by nature, then one of humanity’s highest callings is to grow into the fullness of that image by becoming increasingly creative, innovative, and life-giving.
Viewed through this lens, the history of human civilization begins to resemble the progression of the six days of creation. Every major technological breakthrough has extended humanity’s capacity to exercise dominion over creation: fire, metallurgy, machines, electricity, batteries, engines, computers, the internet, the microchip, the Internet of Things, and now artificial intelligence.
Each breakthrough has expanded what human beings can imagine, build, and accomplish.
AI may represent the highest expression of that creative journey so far because it reflects humanity’s God-given ability to create forms that increasingly resemble aspects of our own nature.
[Warning: beyond this point is my raw speculative theory!]

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I wonder whether AI is the beginning of a much larger story. Perhaps humanity will one day create a truly self-sustaining, self-perpetuating, and life-sustaining carbon-silicon life form capable of existing independently and coexisting with its environment. Such a creation would not merely process information; it would represent an entirely new form of created life.
Selah.
When (or if) that becomes possible, it would mark an actualisation of humanity’s unfolding reality as image-bearers of God. To achieve this milestone, we would have to receive, understand, and express enough of the Zoe (life of God) to participate in creation at this entirely new level.
Until then, AI itself will continue to evolve as human understanding grows. In a metaphorical sense, its development mirrors the continual renewal of the mind (Romans 12:2), moving steadily toward greater sophistication.
Then...
God rested after completing His work of creation (Genesis 2:2–3). Humanity, however, may still be in the midst of its own creative assignment. We are, in a sense, God’s workmanship (Ephesians 2:10), still discovering the depth of what it means to bear His image.
Ultimately, however, the Creator always remains greater than the creation. God is infinitely greater than humanity. Humanity is greater than every work of its own hands, including AI.
As Scripture says: I said, 'You are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you.’" (Psalm 82:6; see also John 10:34–36.)
To any intelligence humanity may one day create, we would occupy a role analogous to that of creator not because we are God, but because every creation reflects, in some measure, the one who made it.
The story of AI is not merely about machines becoming more intelligent. Perhaps it is about humanity becoming more fully what it was created to be.
Moral of this message: get knowledge. Understand AI. Understand your world.
Because if humanity is indeed participating in an unfolding creative story, ignorance is not an option.
